1:
"But of the times and the seasons, brethren, ye have no need that I write unto you." The witnesses note that the times and seasons need no further writing. Chrysostom notes that nothing is so curious and prone to pry into things obscure as the nature of man, especially when the mind is unsettled — like children forever teasing with questions; Paul checks this idle curiosity about dates. The witnesses note Paul saying that, concerning the times and seasons, they had no need that he write to them; the curiosity about dates checked, the certainty of the event affirmed — that Paul, having comforted them about the dead at Christ's coming, turns to the question of "when," and declines to feed idle speculation about the precise times and seasons of that day; not because such knowledge would profit them, but because the day's certainty (not its date) is what matters, and of the manner of its coming they were already sufficiently taught; of the times and the seasons... ye have no need that I write. The witnesses note of the times and the seasons. The witnesses note ye have no need that I write unto you.

The witnesses note that the times and seasons need no further writing — Paul checking idle curiosity about dates, the day's certainty mattering more than its date.

So the witnesses make the verse the checking of curiosity about dates. The lesson the expositors draw is that the precise times need no telling, the certainty of the day mattering more than its date. The witnesses note the times and seasons: "of the times and the seasons" — the question of "when" the day comes; the times and seasons; the the when; the dates; the the day; the question. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the curiosity prone to pry into obscure things, like teasing children; the curiosity; the pry; the obscure; the children; the idle. The witnesses press the no need: "ye have no need that I write unto you" — they had no need that Paul write of it; the no need; the no writing; the no telling; the sufficient; the already taught. The expositors draw the lesson on the idle speculation: Paul declines to feed idle speculation about precise dates; the idle speculation; the precise dates; the declines; the not profit; the curiosity. The witnesses observe the certainty not date: the day's certainty, not its date, is what matters; the certainty not date; the the day; the certain; the the date; the matters. The expositors note the already taught: of the manner they were already sufficiently taught; the already taught; the the manner; the sufficient; the no need; the instructed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of the times and the seasons, the ye have no need that I write unto you, and the curiosity checked. The verse thus records the checking of curiosity about dates — teaching that the precise times need no telling, the certainty of the day mattering more than its date.
2:
"For yourselves know perfectly that the day of the Lord so cometh as a thief in the night." The witnesses note the day of the Lord coming unexpectedly as a thief. Gill notes they knew perfectly — with great exactness and clearness, as a certain truth made plain to them — this they had either from the words of Christ himself or from the apostle's ministry. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they knew perfectly that the day of the Lord comes as a thief in the night; the certainty of the day and the suddenness of its coming — that Paul, instead of dates, gives them what they already knew well: that "the day of the Lord" (the day of his coming and judgment) comes "as a thief in the night" — that is, suddenly, unexpectedly, and at an hour none can foresee, taking the careless by surprise as a thief breaks in when men sleep, so that watchfulness, not date-setting, is the believer's wisdom; the day of the Lord so cometh as a thief in the night. The witnesses note the day of the Lord so cometh. The witnesses note as a thief in the night.

The witnesses note the day of the Lord coming as a thief in the night — suddenly, unexpectedly, at an hour none can foresee; watchfulness, not date-setting, the believer's wisdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the sudden coming of the day. The lesson the expositors draw is that the day comes suddenly and unforeseen, so watchfulness is the believer's wisdom. The witnesses note the know perfectly: "yourselves know perfectly" — Gill notes they knew it perfectly, a certain truth; the know perfectly; the certain truth; the made plain; the from Christ; the the apostle. The expositors observe the day of the Lord: "that the day of the Lord so cometh" — the day of the Lord (his coming and judgment); the the day of the Lord; the his coming; the judgment; the the day; the comes. The witnesses press the thief: "as a thief in the night" — as a thief in the night (suddenly, unexpectedly); the as a thief; the in the night; the suddenly; the unexpectedly; the surprise. The expositors draw the lesson on the unforeseen hour: the day comes at an hour none can foresee, taking the careless by surprise; the unforeseen hour; the none can foresee; the the careless; the surprise; the sudden. The witnesses observe the breaks in when men sleep: as a thief breaks in when men sleep, so the day surprises the unready; the breaks in; the men sleep; the surprises; the the unready; the sudden. The expositors note the watchfulness: watchfulness, not date-setting, is the believer's wisdom; the watchfulness; the not date-setting; the the wisdom; the watch; the ready. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the day of the Lord so cometh, the as a thief in the night, and the sudden coming. The verse thus records the sudden coming of the day — teaching that the day comes suddenly and unforeseen, so watchfulness is the believer's wisdom.
3:
"For when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction cometh upon them, as travail upon a woman with child; and they shall not escape." The witnesses note the sudden destruction overtaking the secure. Gill notes "they" are wicked and ungodly men, persons in a state of unregeneracy, who cry "peace and safety" while careless of the coming judgment. The witnesses note Paul declaring that when they say "peace and safety," then sudden destruction comes upon them as travail on a woman with child, and they shall not escape; the false security of the wicked broken by inescapable ruin — that Paul shows the terror the day holds for the unready: just when the ungodly are crying "peace and safety," flattering themselves that all is well and no judgment near, "sudden destruction" falls upon them — as inevitable and as suddenly as the birth-pangs that seize a pregnant woman, allowing no escape — so that their very security is the prelude to their ruin, and there is no fleeing the day for those it surprises; sudden destruction cometh upon them... and they shall not escape. The witnesses note when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction. The witnesses note they shall not escape.

The witnesses note the sudden destruction overtaking the secure — just as the wicked cry "peace and safety," inescapable ruin falls, as birth-pangs seize a woman.

So the witnesses make the verse the false security of the wicked broken by ruin. The lesson the expositors draw is that sudden, inescapable destruction overtakes the ungodly in their very security. The witnesses note the peace and safety: "when they shall say, Peace and safety" — Gill notes the ungodly cry peace and safety, careless of judgment; the peace and safety; the the ungodly; the careless; the flattering; the secure. The expositors observe the sudden destruction: "then sudden destruction cometh upon them" — sudden destruction falls upon them; the sudden destruction; the falls; the upon them; the ruin; the the day. The witnesses press the travail: "as travail upon a woman with child" — as inevitable as the birth-pangs of a pregnant woman; the travail; the birth-pangs; the a woman; the inevitable; the sudden. The expositors draw the lesson on the inescapable: "and they shall not escape" — and they shall not escape; the inescapable; the not escape; the no fleeing; the the day; the surprised. The witnesses observe the security the prelude: their very security is the prelude to their ruin; the security the prelude; the very security; the the ruin; the prelude; the secure. The expositors note the terror for the unready: the day holds terror for the unready it surprises; the terror; the the unready; the surprises; the the day; the ruin. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when they shall say Peace and safety then sudden destruction, the they shall not escape, and the false security broken. The verse thus records the false security of the wicked broken by ruin — teaching that sudden, inescapable destruction overtakes the ungodly in their very security.
4:
"But ye, brethren, are not in darkness, that that day should overtake you as a thief." The witnesses note that believers, not in darkness, are not surprised by the day. Gill notes a state of unregeneracy is a state of darkness, blindness, and ignorance, the condition of all men by nature, who are born in darkness, walk in it, and dwell in it; but believers are delivered from it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they, the brethren, are not in darkness, that the day should overtake them as a thief; the believer's deliverance from the darkness that the day surprises — that Paul draws the comforting contrast: unlike the careless wicked who walk in the darkness of unbelief and sin and are caught unawares, the believers "are not in darkness" — they have been brought into the light of Christ and the knowledge of the truth, are awake and watching — so that "that day" shall not "overtake" them "as a thief," surprising and destroying them; for the children of light are forewarned and forearmed; ye, brethren, are not in darkness. The witnesses note ye, brethren, are not in darkness. The witnesses note that that day should overtake you as a thief.

The witnesses note that believers, not in darkness, are not surprised by the day — brought into the light, awake and watching, the children of light forewarned.

So the witnesses make the verse the believer's deliverance from surprise. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers, brought into the light, are not caught unawares by the day as the wicked are. The witnesses note the not in darkness: "ye, brethren, are not in darkness" — Gill notes believers are delivered from the darkness of unregeneracy; the not in darkness; the delivered; the unregeneracy; the the light; the awake. The expositors observe the state of darkness: a state of unregeneracy is darkness, blindness, ignorance; the state of darkness; the unregeneracy; the blindness; the ignorance; the nature. The witnesses press the not overtaken: "that that day should overtake you as a thief" — the day shall not overtake them as a thief; the not overtaken; the the day; the as a thief; the surprise; the not caught. The expositors draw the lesson on the light: believers have been brought into the light of Christ and the truth; the the light; the of Christ; the the truth; the brought; the awake. The witnesses observe the awake and watching: they are awake and watching, not careless; the awake; the watching; the not careless; the alert; the ready. The expositors note the forewarned: the children of light are forewarned and forearmed; the forewarned; the forearmed; the children of light; the ready; the watching. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye brethren are not in darkness, the that that day should overtake you as a thief, and the deliverance from surprise. The verse thus records the believer's deliverance from surprise — teaching that believers, brought into the light, are not caught unawares by the day.
5:
"Ye are all the children of light, and the children of the day: we are not of the night, nor of darkness." The witnesses note the believers as children of light and of the day. Gill notes they are enlightened persons, whose understandings were enlightened by the Spirit of God to see their lost state, the sinfulness of sin, the insufficiency of their own righteousness, and the excellency of Christ's righteousness and the way of salvation by him. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they are all the children of light and of the day, not of the night nor of darkness; the bright character of all true believers as light's children — that Paul presses the contrast home: all the believers, without exception, are "children of light" and "children of the day" — born of the light, belonging to the realm of light and truth, enlightened by the Spirit — and emphatically "not of the night, nor of darkness," having no part in the ignorance, sin, and false security that mark the unconverted, so that their whole nature and standing fit them for the day, not the dark; ye are all the children of light, and the children of the day. The witnesses note ye are all the children of light, and the children of the day. The witnesses note we are not of the night, nor of darkness.

The witnesses note all believers as children of light and of the day — born of the light, enlightened by the Spirit, not of the night nor of darkness.

So the witnesses make the verse the bright character of believers. The lesson the expositors draw is that all believers belong to the light and the day, not to the night and darkness. The witnesses note the children of light: "ye are all the children of light, and the children of the day" — Gill notes they are enlightened by the Spirit; the children of light; the children of the day; the enlightened; the the Spirit; the light. The expositors observe the enlightened: their understandings were enlightened to see their lost state and Christ's righteousness; the enlightened; the their state; the Christ's righteousness; the the way; the salvation. The witnesses press the not of night: "we are not of the night, nor of darkness" — not of the night nor darkness; the not of night; the not darkness; the no part; the the unconverted; the light. The expositors draw the lesson on belonging to light: they belong to the realm of light and truth; the belonging to light; the the realm; the light; the truth; the born of. The witnesses observe the no part in darkness: they have no part in the ignorance, sin, and false security of the unconverted; the no part; the ignorance; the sin; the false security; the the unconverted. The expositors note the fit for the day: their whole nature fits them for the day, not the dark; the fit for the day; the their nature; the the day; the not the dark; the standing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye are all the children of light and the children of the day, the we are not of the night nor of darkness, and the bright character. The verse thus records the bright character of believers — teaching that all believers belong to the light and the day, not to the night and darkness.
6:
"Therefore let us not sleep, as do others; but let us watch and be sober." The witnesses note the call to watchfulness and sobriety. Gill notes "let us not sleep as do others" — as the rest of the Gentiles, unconverted persons in a state of darkness, children of the night; professors too much resemble them when they indulge carnal lusts and are careless about Christ's coming. The witnesses note Paul exhorting that they not sleep as others, but watch and be sober; the duty of the children of light to watch and be sober — that Paul draws the practical lesson from their bright character: since they are of the day, they must not "sleep" (live in carnal security, spiritual drowsiness, and indifference) "as do others" (the unconverted, and the careless professor), but must "watch" (be spiritually alert and on guard for Christ's coming) and "be sober" (temperate, self-controlled, clear-minded, free from the intoxication of the world's cares and pleasures), as becomes those who belong to the day; let us watch and be sober. The witnesses note let us not sleep, as do others. The witnesses note but let us watch and be sober.

The witnesses note the call to watch and be sober — not to sleep in carnal security as others, but to be spiritually alert and self-controlled, as becomes the children of the day.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of watchfulness and sobriety. The lesson the expositors draw is that the children of the day must not sleep in carnal security, but watch and be sober. The witnesses note the not sleep: "let us not sleep, as do others" — Gill notes not to sleep as the unconverted; the not sleep; the as do others; the the unconverted; the carnal security; the drowsiness. The expositors observe the careless professor: even professors resemble the careless when they indulge lusts; the careless professor; the indulge lusts; the careless; the resemble; the the unconverted. The witnesses press the watch: "but let us watch" — but watch (be spiritually alert and on guard); the watch; the alert; the on guard; the for his coming; the awake. The expositors draw the lesson on the sober: "and be sober" — and be sober (temperate, self-controlled, clear-minded); the sober; the temperate; the self-controlled; the clear-minded; the free. The witnesses observe the not intoxicated: free from the intoxication of the world's cares and pleasures; the not intoxicated; the the world's cares; the pleasures; the free; the clear. The expositors note the becomes the day: this becomes those who belong to the day; the becomes the day; the belong to the day; the their character; the fitting; the day. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us not sleep as do others, the but let us watch and be sober, and the duty of watchfulness. The verse thus records the duty of watchfulness and sobriety — teaching that the children of the day must not sleep in carnal security, but watch and be sober.
7:
"For they that sleep sleep in the night; and they that be drunken are drunken in the night." The witnesses note that sleep and drunkenness belong to the night. Gill notes the night is the usual season for sleep, and sleep is only for those who are in darkness and children of the night, not proper to be indulged by children of the day. Barnes notes the day is the time for action, the night for sleep. The witnesses note Paul observing that those who sleep sleep in the night, and those who are drunken are drunken in the night; the works of darkness belonging to the night, not the day — that Paul supports his exhortation with a plain truth from common life: sleeping and drunkenness are the deeds of night-time — men sleep and grow drunk under cover of darkness — and so, by analogy, spiritual sleep (carelessness) and spiritual drunkenness (indulgence in worldly pleasures) belong to the night of unbelief and sin, and are utterly unfitting for those who are children of the day; they that sleep sleep in the night. The witnesses note they that sleep sleep in the night. The witnesses note they that be drunken are drunken in the night.

The witnesses note that sleep and drunkenness belong to the night — so spiritual sleep and worldly indulgence belong to the night of sin, unfitting for the children of the day.

So the witnesses make the verse the works of darkness belonging to the night. The lesson the expositors draw is that spiritual sleep and drunkenness belong to the night of sin, unfitting for the children of the day. The witnesses note the sleep in night: "they that sleep sleep in the night" — Gill notes the night is the season for sleep, for the children of the night; the sleep in night; the the night; the the season; the children of night; the not the day. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes the day is for action, the night for sleep; the day for action; the night for sleep; the contrast; the the day; the the night. The witnesses press the drunken in night: "they that be drunken are drunken in the night" — drunkenness too is a deed of night; the drunken; the in the night; the the deed; the under cover; the darkness. The expositors draw the lesson on the analogy: by analogy, spiritual sleep and drunkenness belong to the night of unbelief; the analogy; the spiritual sleep; the spiritual drunkenness; the the night; the unbelief. The witnesses observe the worldly pleasures: spiritual drunkenness is indulgence in worldly pleasures; the worldly pleasures; the indulgence; the spiritual drunkenness; the the night; the sin. The expositors note the unfitting for the day: these are utterly unfitting for the children of the day; the unfitting; the the day; the children; the not fitting; the the night. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they that sleep sleep in the night, the they that be drunken are drunken in the night, and the works of darkness. The verse thus records the works of darkness belonging to the night — teaching that spiritual sleep and drunkenness belong to the night of sin, unfitting for the children of the day.
8:
"But let us, who are of the day, be sober, putting on the breastplate of faith and love; and for an helmet, the hope of salvation." The witnesses note the call to sobriety and the armour of faith, love, and hope. Gill notes that we who are of the day are to be sober — to cast off the works of darkness, having no fellowship with them, and to walk as children of the light, living soberly, righteously, and godly. The witnesses note Paul exhorting that they who are of the day be sober, putting on the breastplate of faith and love, and for a helmet the hope of salvation; the watchful sobriety armed with faith, love, and hope — that Paul, returning to the duty of sobriety, arms the watching believer for the day with the great Christian graces, cast as a soldier's armour: "the breastplate of faith and love" guarding the heart, and "the hope of salvation" as a helmet covering the head, so that the three abiding graces — faith, love, and hope — are the very defence by which the child of the day stands ready and unsurprised for the coming of the Lord; the breastplate of faith and love; and for an helmet, the hope of salvation. The witnesses note let us, who are of the day, be sober. The witnesses note putting on the breastplate of faith and love; and for an helmet, the hope of salvation.

The witnesses note the call to sobriety armed with faith, love, and hope — the breastplate of faith and love, the helmet of the hope of salvation; the watching believer's defence.

So the witnesses make the verse the watchful sobriety armed with the graces. The lesson the expositors draw is that the child of the day is to be sober, armed with faith, love, and hope as his defence. The witnesses note the sober: "let us, who are of the day, be sober" — Gill notes those of the day are to be sober, casting off darkness; the sober; the of the day; the cast off; the darkness; the soberly. The expositors observe the breastplate: "putting on the breastplate of faith and love" — the breastplate of faith and love guarding the heart; the breastplate; the faith and love; the the heart; the guarding; the armour. The witnesses press the helmet: "and for an helmet, the hope of salvation" — the hope of salvation as a helmet for the head; the helmet; the the hope; the salvation; the the head; the covering. The expositors draw the lesson on the three graces: the three abiding graces — faith, love, hope — are cast as armour; the three graces; the faith; the love; the hope; the armour. The witnesses observe the defence: these graces are the defence by which the believer stands ready; the defence; the the graces; the stands ready; the the believer; the armed. The expositors note the unsurprised: armed thus, the child of the day is unsurprised for the coming; the unsurprised; the the child of day; the the coming; the ready; the armed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us who are of the day be sober, the putting on the breastplate of faith and love and for an helmet the hope of salvation, and the armour of graces. The verse thus records the watchful sobriety armed with the graces — teaching that the child of the day is to be sober, armed with faith, love, and hope as his defence.
9:
"For God hath not appointed us to wrath, but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ," The witnesses note that God has appointed his people not to wrath but to salvation. Gill notes God has not appointed his people to wrath and destruction; though there are vessels of wrath fitted for destruction, ordained of old to condemnation, there are others who, though equally children of wrath in themselves, are not appointed to it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God has not appointed us to wrath, but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ; the sovereign appointment of his people to salvation, not wrath — that Paul gives the deepest ground of the believer's hope and security against the day of wrath: God's own eternal appointment, which has destined his people not "to wrath" (the condemnation and ruin that fall on the ungodly) but "to obtain salvation" — and that "by our Lord Jesus Christ," through his mediation and work — so that the believer's escape from the coming destruction rests not on himself but on God's sovereign, gracious purpose in Christ; God hath not appointed us to wrath, but to obtain salvation. The witnesses note God hath not appointed us to wrath. The witnesses note but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note God appointing his people not to wrath but to salvation by Christ — the believer's escape resting on God's sovereign, gracious purpose, not himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointment to salvation, not wrath. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer's hope rests on God's eternal appointment to salvation by Christ. The witnesses note the not to wrath: "God hath not appointed us to wrath" — Gill notes God has not appointed his people to wrath; the not to wrath; the not destruction; the not condemnation; the appointed; the destined. The expositors observe the children of wrath: though equally children of wrath in themselves, they are not appointed to it; the children of wrath; the in themselves; the equally; the not appointed; the spared. The witnesses press the to salvation: "but to obtain salvation" — but to obtain salvation; the to salvation; the obtain; the destined; the appointed; the saved. The expositors draw the lesson on by Christ: "by our Lord Jesus Christ" — by his mediation and work; the by Christ; the his mediation; the his work; the through him; the the means. The witnesses observe the deepest ground: this is the deepest ground of the believer's hope; the deepest ground; the the hope; the security; the the day of wrath; the firm. The expositors note the sovereign purpose: the escape rests on God's sovereign, gracious purpose; the sovereign purpose; the gracious; the not himself; the God's; the appointment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God hath not appointed us to wrath, the but to obtain salvation by our Lord Jesus Christ, and the appointment to salvation. The verse thus records the appointment to salvation, not wrath — teaching that the believer's hope rests on God's eternal appointment to salvation by Christ.
10:
"Who died for us, that, whether we wake or sleep, we should live together with him." The witnesses note Christ's death securing life with him, waking or sleeping. Gill notes Christ died for the elect, who are not appointed to wrath but to salvation — not merely as a martyr or example, but as a surety, in their room and stead, as a sacrifice for their sins, to make atonement and save them. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ died for us, that whether we wake or sleep, we should live together with him; the death of Christ the ground of the believer's life, in death or life — that Paul roots the appointed salvation in the atoning death of Christ, who "died for us" (in the believer's place, as a sacrifice for sin), with this end: that "whether we wake or sleep" (whether found alive at his coming or already fallen asleep in death) "we should live together with him" — that is, all his people, living and dead alike, shall share one common life and eternal fellowship with him, so that death cannot sever the believer from the life Christ purchased; that, whether we wake or sleep, we should live together with him. The witnesses note who died for us. The witnesses note that, whether we wake or sleep, we should live together with him.

The witnesses note Christ's death securing that, whether waking or sleeping, his people live together with him — death cannot sever them from the life Christ purchased.

So the witnesses make the verse the death of Christ the ground of life with him. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ's atoning death secures that all his people, living and dead, shall live together with him. The witnesses note the died for us: "who died for us" — Gill notes Christ died as a surety, a sacrifice, in their stead; the died for us; the surety; the sacrifice; the in their stead; the atonement. The expositors observe the not a martyr only: not merely as a martyr or example, but as a sacrifice for sin; the not a martyr; the not example only; the sacrifice; the atonement; the substitute. The witnesses press the wake or sleep: "that, whether we wake or sleep" — whether found alive or already asleep in death; the wake or sleep; the alive; the asleep; the living and dead; the either. The expositors draw the lesson on live with him: "we should live together with him" — all his people share one life and fellowship with him; the live together; the with him; the one life; the fellowship; the eternal. The witnesses observe the living and dead alike: living and dead alike share his life; the living and dead; the alike; the share; the his life; the together. The expositors note the death cannot sever: death cannot sever the believer from the life Christ purchased; the death cannot sever; the the life; the purchased; the secure; the with him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who died for us, the that whether we wake or sleep we should live together with him, and the death securing life. The verse thus records the death of Christ the ground of life with him — teaching that Christ's atoning death secures that all his people, living and dead, shall live together with him.
11:
"Wherefore comfort yourselves together, and edify one another, even as also ye do." The witnesses note the call to mutual comfort and edification. Gill notes they were to comfort one another with the doctrine of the resurrection, the second coming of Christ, and being forever with him, or with the consideration that they were not in darkness but children of the light, appointed not to wrath but to salvation. The witnesses note Paul charging them to comfort themselves together and edify one another, even as they were already doing; the duty of mutual comfort and building up, already practised — that Paul, drawing the practical use from the great truths of salvation and the coming, bids the believers "comfort" (console and cheer) one another with these doctrines and "edify" (build up in faith and holiness) one another, and graciously adds, "even as also ye do" — commending the practice already among them while urging its continuance, so that the church is to be a place of mutual encouragement and growth; comfort yourselves together, and edify one another. The witnesses note comfort yourselves together, and edify one another. The witnesses note even as also ye do.

The witnesses note the call to comfort and edify one another — building up in faith with the great truths of salvation and the coming, a practice already among them.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of mutual comfort and edification. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers are to console and build up one another with the truths of salvation and Christ's coming. The witnesses note the comfort: "comfort yourselves together" — Gill notes comfort with the doctrine of the resurrection and the coming; the comfort; the together; the console; the cheer; the the doctrines. The expositors observe the truths: comfort with salvation, the coming, being forever with Christ; the truths; the salvation; the the coming; the forever with Christ; the doctrines. The witnesses press the edify: "and edify one another" — and edify (build up in faith and holiness); the edify; the build up; the one another; the faith; the holiness. The expositors draw the lesson on the mutual: the believers are to console and build up one another; the mutual; the console; the build up; the one another; the church. The witnesses observe the already doing: "even as also ye do" — commending the practice already among them; the already doing; the commending; the practised; the among them; the continue. The expositors note the place of encouragement: the church is to be a place of mutual encouragement and growth; the place of encouragement; the mutual; the growth; the the church; the build up. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the comfort yourselves together and edify one another, the even as also ye do, and the mutual comfort. The verse thus records the duty of mutual comfort and edification — teaching that believers are to console and build up one another with the truths of salvation and Christ's coming.
12:
"And we beseech you, brethren, to know them which labour among you, and are over you in the Lord, and admonish you;" The witnesses note the duty to acknowledge those who labour as pastors. Chrysostom notes a ruler must have many occasions of enmity, as physicians give trouble to the sick with unpleasant diet and medicines, and fathers are often annoying to their children; so teachers, and much more, yet for benefit. The witnesses note Paul beseeching them to know those who labour among them, are over them in the Lord, and admonish them; the honour due to faithful, labouring pastors — that Paul turns to the believers' duty toward their ministers, asking them to "know" (acknowledge, regard, value) those who serve them — describing such pastors by their threefold work: they "labour among you" (toil hard in teaching and ministry), are "over you in the Lord" (set as rulers and overseers, by the Lord's appointment, in spiritual things), and "admonish you" (warn, reprove, and instruct) — so that the people are to recognize and esteem the office and labour of their faithful guides; know them which labour among you, and are over you in the Lord. The witnesses note know them which labour among you. The witnesses note are over you in the Lord, and admonish you.

The witnesses note the duty to acknowledge faithful pastors — who labour, are over them in the Lord, and admonish them; the people to recognize and esteem their guides.

So the witnesses make the verse the honour due to faithful pastors. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people are to acknowledge and value those who labour, rule, and admonish among them. The witnesses note the know: "to know them which labour among you" — Paul asks them to know (acknowledge, value) their ministers; the know; the acknowledge; the value; the regard; the ministers. The expositors observe the labour: "which labour among you" — they labour (toil hard in teaching and ministry); the labour; the toil; the teaching; the ministry; the hard. The witnesses press the over you: "and are over you in the Lord" — set as rulers and overseers in spiritual things, by the Lord; the over you; the rulers; the overseers; the in the Lord; the appointed. The expositors draw the lesson on the admonish: "and admonish you" — they admonish (warn, reprove, instruct); the admonish; the warn; the reprove; the instruct; the the people. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the ruler, like a physician or father, may give trouble for benefit; the the ruler; the physician; the father; the trouble; the benefit. The expositors note the recognize and esteem: the people are to recognize and esteem their faithful guides; the recognize; the esteem; the the office; the the labour; the guides. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the know them which labour among you, the are over you in the Lord and admonish you, and the honour due to pastors. The verse thus records the honour due to faithful pastors — teaching that the people are to acknowledge and value those who labour, rule, and admonish among them.
13:
"And to esteem them very highly in love for their work's sake. And be at peace among yourselves." The witnesses note the high esteem due to pastors and the call to peace. Gill notes they are to be esteemed very highly — honoured abundantly, had in reputation, high in the affections of the saints, who should esteem them better than themselves, and speak well of them, for their work's sake. The witnesses note Paul charging them to esteem their pastors very highly in love for their work's sake, and to be at peace among themselves; the loving esteem of ministers and the peace of the church — that Paul completes the duty toward pastors: they are to be held in the very highest esteem, and that "in love" (with warm, heartfelt affection, not cold deference) and "for their work's sake" (because of the worth and importance of the gospel labour they do, not for personal reasons) — and he joins to this the broader charge, "be at peace among yourselves," that the whole church live in mutual harmony, which is itself bound up with right regard for its leaders; esteem them very highly in love for their work's sake. The witnesses note esteem them very highly in love for their work's sake. The witnesses note be at peace among yourselves.

The witnesses note the high, loving esteem due to pastors for their work's sake, and the call to be at peace among themselves — the harmony of the whole church.

So the witnesses make the verse the esteem of pastors and the peace of the church. The lesson the expositors draw is that pastors are to be highly esteemed in love for their work, and the church to live in peace. The witnesses note the esteem highly: "to esteem them very highly" — Gill notes they are to be esteemed very highly, honoured abundantly; the esteem highly; the abundantly; the reputation; the affection; the honoured. The expositors observe the in love: "in love" — with warm, heartfelt affection, not cold deference; the in love; the affection; the heartfelt; the warm; the not cold. The witnesses press the for their work: "for their work's sake" — because of the worth of their gospel labour; the for their work; the the worth; the gospel labour; the importance; the not personal. The expositors draw the lesson on the loving esteem: the loving esteem of ministers for their work; the loving esteem; the ministers; the for their work; the affection; the honour. The witnesses observe the peace: "be at peace among yourselves" — be at peace among yourselves; the peace; the among yourselves; the harmony; the the church; the mutual. The expositors note the bound up: the church's peace is bound up with right regard for its leaders; the bound up; the the peace; the right regard; the leaders; the harmony. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the esteem them very highly in love for their work's sake, the be at peace among yourselves, and the esteem and peace. The verse thus records the esteem of pastors and the peace of the church — teaching that pastors are to be highly esteemed in love for their work, and the church to live in peace.
14:
"Now we exhort you, brethren, warn them that are unruly, comfort the feebleminded, support the weak, be patient toward all men." The witnesses note the fourfold charge of dealing wisely with various souls. Gill notes this is said either to the ministers who laboured among them and presided over them, or to the members in conjunction with their pastors, in the mutual duties of the body. The witnesses note Paul exhorting them to warn the unruly, comfort the feebleminded, support the weak, and be patient toward all; the wise and tender care suited to each kind of soul — that Paul gives a fourfold rule of pastoral and brotherly care, fitting the remedy to the need: the "unruly" (the disorderly, those out of rank) are to be "warned" (admonished, reproved); the "feebleminded" (the fainthearted, the discouraged) are to be "comforted" (cheered and encouraged); the "weak" (those weak in faith or grace) are to be "supported" (upheld, held up, borne with); and toward "all men" they are to be "patient" (longsuffering, forbearing), so that each soul receives the treatment its case requires; warn the unruly, comfort the feebleminded, support the weak. The witnesses note warn them that are unruly, comfort the feebleminded, support the weak. The witnesses note be patient toward all men.

The witnesses note the fourfold charge — warn the unruly, comfort the feebleminded, support the weak, be patient toward all — fitting the remedy to each soul's need.

So the witnesses make the verse the wise care suited to each soul. The lesson the expositors draw is that pastoral and brotherly care fits the remedy to the need, dealing differently with different souls. The witnesses note the warn the unruly: "warn them that are unruly" — the unruly (disorderly) are to be warned; the warn; the the unruly; the disorderly; the admonished; the reproved. The expositors observe the comfort the feebleminded: "comfort the feebleminded" — the fainthearted are to be comforted; the comfort; the feebleminded; the fainthearted; the discouraged; the cheered. The witnesses press the support the weak: "support the weak" — the weak (in faith or grace) are to be supported; the support; the the weak; the in faith; the upheld; the borne. The expositors draw the lesson on the be patient: "be patient toward all men" — toward all, be patient (longsuffering); the patient; the toward all; the longsuffering; the forbearing; the all. The witnesses observe the fit the remedy: each soul receives the treatment its case requires; the fit the remedy; the each soul; the the treatment; the the case; the suited. The expositors note the fourfold rule: this is a fourfold rule of pastoral and brotherly care; the fourfold rule; the pastoral; the brotherly; the care; the various. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the warn them that are unruly comfort the feebleminded support the weak, the be patient toward all men, and the wise care. The verse thus records the wise care suited to each soul — teaching that pastoral and brotherly care fits the remedy to the need.
15:
"See that none render evil for evil unto any man; but ever follow that which is good, both among yourselves, and to all men." The witnesses note the charge against revenge and toward doing good to all. Gill notes none is to render evil for evil — not an ill word for an ill word, railing for railing, nor an ill action for an ill action — and not to any man whatever, neither enemy nor persecutor, as well as not to a brother. The witnesses note Paul charging that none render evil for evil to any man, but ever follow what is good, among themselves and to all; the forbidding of revenge and the pursuit of good to all — that Paul forbids the natural impulse of retaliation: the believer is to "see" (take care) that he renders no "evil for evil" to "any man" (not even to an enemy or persecutor), but instead is "ever" (always) to "follow" (pursue, seek after) "that which is good" — actively doing good — and that not only "among yourselves" (within the church) but "to all men" (even to those without and to enemies), so that the Christian overcomes evil not by revenge but by persistent goodness; ever follow that which is good... to all men. The witnesses note see that none render evil for evil unto any man. The witnesses note ever follow that which is good... both among yourselves, and to all men.

The witnesses note the charge against revenge — render no evil for evil to any man — and toward ever pursuing good to all; overcoming evil by persistent goodness.

So the witnesses make the verse the forbidding of revenge and the pursuit of good. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer renders no evil for evil but ever pursues good, to all men. The witnesses note the no evil for evil: "see that none render evil for evil unto any man" — Gill notes no ill word or action returned, to no man; the no evil for evil; the no ill word; the no ill action; the to no man; the not retaliate. The expositors observe the not even enemies: not even to an enemy or persecutor; the not even enemies; the enemy; the persecutor; the not a brother; the any man. The witnesses press the follow good: "but ever follow that which is good" — ever (always) follow (pursue) what is good; the follow good; the ever; the always; the pursue; the seek. The expositors draw the lesson on the active good: the believer is to actively do good; the active good; the doing good; the pursue; the seek; the good. The witnesses observe the to all: "both among yourselves, and to all men" — among themselves and to all men (even enemies); the to all; the among yourselves; the all men; the even enemies; the without. The expositors note the overcoming evil: the Christian overcomes evil not by revenge but by persistent goodness; the overcoming evil; the not revenge; the goodness; the persistent; the good. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the see that none render evil for evil unto any man, the ever follow that which is good both among yourselves and to all men, and the pursuit of good. The verse thus records the forbidding of revenge and the pursuit of good — teaching that the believer renders no evil for evil but ever pursues good, to all men.
16:
"Rejoice evermore." The witnesses note the brief but sweeping command to rejoice always. Gill notes this rejoicing is not carnal but spiritual — joy in the Holy Ghost, arising from a view of pardon by the blood of Christ, justification by his righteousness, and atonement by his sacrifice, not a joy in oneself or in the world. The witnesses note Paul commanding them to rejoice evermore; the call to a constant spiritual joy — that Paul, in the briefest of commands, enjoins a perpetual gladness: not a fleeting or worldly mirth, but the deep, abiding "joy in the Holy Ghost" that springs from the believer's standing in Christ — his pardon, justification, and reconciliation — a joy grounded not in changing circumstances but in Christ and his unchanging grace, and therefore to be maintained "evermore," in every condition; rejoice evermore. The witnesses note rejoice. The witnesses note evermore.

The witnesses note the sweeping command to rejoice always — not a worldly mirth but the abiding joy in the Holy Ghost, grounded in Christ and his unchanging grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to constant spiritual joy. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to maintain a perpetual gladness grounded in Christ, not circumstances. The witnesses note the rejoice: "rejoice" — Paul commands them to rejoice; the rejoice; the gladness; the joy; the command; the be glad. The expositors observe the spiritual joy: Gill notes this is spiritual joy, joy in the Holy Ghost; the spiritual joy; the in the Holy Ghost; the not carnal; the deep; the abiding. The witnesses press the springs from Christ: the joy springs from pardon, justification, and atonement in Christ; the springs from Christ; the pardon; the justification; the atonement; the his grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the evermore: "evermore" — always, perpetually, in every condition; the evermore; the always; the perpetually; the every condition; the maintained. The witnesses observe the not circumstances: the joy is grounded not in changing circumstances but in Christ; the not circumstances; the changing; the in Christ; the unchanging grace; the grounded. The expositors note the brief command: in the briefest of commands Paul enjoins perpetual gladness; the brief command; the perpetual; the gladness; the enjoins; the sweeping. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rejoice, the evermore, and the constant joy. The verse thus records the call to constant spiritual joy — teaching that the believer is to maintain a perpetual gladness grounded in Christ, not circumstances.
17:
"Pray without ceasing." The witnesses note the command to constant, unceasing prayer. Gill notes this does not mean saints should be always on their knees or ever vocally calling on God — which would clash with other duties of worship and civil life, and be impracticable — but to pray constantly, frequently, and with a praying frame of heart maintained. The witnesses note Paul commanding them to pray without ceasing; the call to a constant spirit and practice of prayer — that Paul, in another brief but weighty word, enjoins unwearied prayer: not the literal impossibility of praying every moment without pause, but a steady perseverance in prayer — praying regularly and often, never giving it up, and keeping the heart always in a prayerful, dependent frame, ready to call on God at all times — so that prayer is the believer's continual breath and habit, not an occasional act; pray without ceasing. The witnesses note pray. The witnesses note without ceasing.

The witnesses note the command to pray without ceasing — not literally every moment, but steady perseverance in prayer, the heart kept in a constant prayerful frame.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to unceasing prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to persevere constantly in prayer, keeping a prayerful frame of heart. The witnesses note the pray: "pray" — Paul commands them to pray; the pray; the prayer; the call on God; the the command; the seek. The expositors observe Gill's note: Gill notes this is not always on the knees, which would be impracticable; the not always; the on the knees; the impracticable; the not literal; the clash. The witnesses press the without ceasing: "without ceasing" — without ceasing (constantly, frequently); the without ceasing; the constantly; the frequently; the unwearied; the persevering. The expositors draw the lesson on the perseverance: the believer is to persevere in prayer, never giving it up; the perseverance; the persevere; the never give up; the regularly; the often. The witnesses observe the prayerful frame: keeping the heart always in a prayerful, dependent frame; the prayerful frame; the the heart; the dependent; the ready; the always. The expositors note the continual breath: prayer is the believer's continual breath and habit, not an occasional act; the continual breath; the habit; the not occasional; the breath; the constant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the pray, the without ceasing, and the unceasing prayer. The verse thus records the call to unceasing prayer — teaching that the believer is to persevere constantly in prayer, keeping a prayerful frame of heart.
18:
"In every thing give thanks: for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you." The witnesses note the command to give thanks in everything as God's will. Gill notes thanks are to be given to God the Father in the name of Christ, for all things — temporal and spiritual — for our beings and their preservation, food and raiment, the means of grace, and spiritual blessings. The witnesses note Paul charging them to give thanks in everything, for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning them; the duty of thanksgiving in all conditions, grounded in God's will — that Paul completes the trio of brief commands (rejoice, pray, give thanks) with the call to gratitude that knows no exception: "in every thing" — in every condition, prosperous or adverse — the believer is to "give thanks," owning God's hand and goodness even in trials, and Paul grounds the duty in the highest authority, "for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you," so that a thankful spirit in all things is not optional but God's revealed and gracious will for his people; in every thing give thanks. The witnesses note in every thing give thanks. The witnesses note this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you.

The witnesses note the command to give thanks in everything, in every condition — God's revealed will for his people, owning his goodness even in trials.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of thanksgiving in all conditions. The lesson the expositors draw is that thanksgiving in everything is God's revealed will for his people, even in trials. The witnesses note the in everything: "in every thing give thanks" — Gill notes thanks for all things, temporal and spiritual; the in everything; the give thanks; the all things; the temporal; the spiritual. The expositors observe the every condition: in every condition, prosperous or adverse; the every condition; the prosperous; the adverse; the in all; the trials. The witnesses press the owning God's hand: the believer owns God's hand and goodness even in trials; the owning God's hand; the his goodness; the even in trials; the gratitude; the thankful. The expositors draw the lesson on the will of God: "for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you" — this is God's will for them; the will of God; the in Christ Jesus; the concerning you; the revealed; the gracious. The witnesses observe the not optional: a thankful spirit is not optional but God's will; the not optional; the God's will; the revealed; the gracious; the for his people. The expositors note the trio: this completes the trio — rejoice, pray, give thanks; the trio; the rejoice; the pray; the give thanks; the three. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in every thing give thanks, the this is the will of God in Christ Jesus concerning you, and the duty of thanksgiving. The verse thus records the duty of thanksgiving in all conditions — teaching that thanksgiving in everything is God's revealed will for his people, even in trials.
19:
"Quench not the Spirit." The witnesses note the warning against quenching the Spirit. Chrysostom notes a thick mist and darkness is spread over all the earth, and so God has given us a bright lamp, kindling in our souls the grace of the Spirit; but some by an unclean life and unworthy doctrines extinguish that light, like a flame deprived of oil or drenched with water. The witnesses note Paul charging them not to quench the Spirit; the warning against extinguishing the Spirit's light and gifts — that Paul, in a vivid figure, warns against putting out the fire of the Spirit: the Holy Spirit, with his grace, gifts, and influences, is like a holy flame kindled in the soul and the church, which may be "quenched" — damped down or extinguished — whether by sin in the life (which grieves and stifles him), or by despising and suppressing his gifts and operations in others (as the gift of prophecy, v.20), so that the believer is to cherish, not smother, the Spirit's working; quench not the Spirit. The witnesses note quench not. The witnesses note the Spirit.

The witnesses note the warning not to quench the Spirit — the holy flame of his grace and gifts, which may be damped or extinguished by sin or by suppressing his operations.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning against quenching the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Spirit's flame in the soul and church is to be cherished, not smothered by sin or suppression. The witnesses note the quench: "quench not" — Paul warns against quenching (extinguishing); the quench; the extinguish; the put out; the dampen; the smother. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the Spirit's grace is a bright lamp, which some extinguish by unclean life or unworthy doctrines; the the lamp; the the grace; the extinguish; the unclean life; the unworthy doctrines. The witnesses press the the Spirit: "the Spirit" — the Holy Spirit, with his grace, gifts, and influences; the the Spirit; the the grace; the the gifts; the the influences; the holy flame. The expositors draw the lesson on the holy flame: the Spirit is like a holy flame kindled in the soul and church; the holy flame; the kindled; the the soul; the the church; the the fire. The witnesses observe the quenched by sin: the Spirit may be quenched by sin in the life, which grieves and stifles him; the quenched by sin; the sin; the grieves; the stifles; the smothers. The expositors note the suppressing gifts: or by despising and suppressing his gifts in others (as prophecy, v.20); the suppressing gifts; the despising; the his gifts; the in others; the prophecy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the quench not, the the Spirit, and the warning. The verse thus records the warning against quenching the Spirit — teaching that the Spirit's flame is to be cherished, not smothered by sin or suppression.
20:
"Despise not prophesyings." The witnesses note the charge not to despise prophesyings. Gill notes "prophesyings" may mean the prophecies of the Old Testament concerning Christ's person, office, and work, which though fulfilled still have their usefulness; or, more immediately, the gift of prophesying or preaching in the church, which was not to be slighted. The witnesses note Paul charging them not to despise prophesyings; the honour due to the prophetic gift and the preaching of the word — that Paul, applying the warning against quenching the Spirit, forbids the despising of "prophesyings" — the inspired or gifted utterance of God's truth in the church (whether the foretelling and forth-telling of the prophets, or the preaching and expounding of the word) — which some, perhaps prizing the showier gift of tongues, were inclined to slight; instead the gift is to be valued and welcomed, for through it the Spirit instructs and edifies the church; despise not prophesyings. The witnesses note despise not. The witnesses note prophesyings.

The witnesses note the charge not to despise prophesyings — the gifted utterance and preaching of God's word, through which the Spirit instructs and edifies the church.

So the witnesses make the verse the honour due to prophesyings. The lesson the expositors draw is that the prophetic gift and preaching of the word are not to be despised but valued. The witnesses note the despise not: "despise not" — Paul forbids despising; the despise not; the not slight; the not contemn; the valued; the welcomed. The expositors observe the prophesyings: "prophesyings" — Gill notes the prophecies of the Old Testament, or the gift of prophesying/preaching; the prophesyings; the the prophecies; the the gift; the preaching; the the word. The witnesses press the gifted utterance: prophesyings are the inspired or gifted utterance of God's truth; the gifted utterance; the inspired; the God's truth; the in the church; the the word. The expositors draw the lesson on the slighted gift: some, prizing the showier gift of tongues, slighted prophecy; the slighted gift; the the showier; the tongues; the slighted; the despised. The witnesses observe the valued: instead the gift is to be valued and welcomed; the valued; the welcomed; the honoured; the prized; the the gift. The expositors note the Spirit edifies: through it the Spirit instructs and edifies the church; the the Spirit edifies; the instructs; the edifies; the the church; the builds up. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the despise not, the prophesyings, and the honour due. The verse thus records the honour due to prophesyings — teaching that the prophetic gift and preaching of the word are not to be despised but valued.
21:
"Prove all things; hold fast that which is good." The witnesses note the call to test all and hold to the good. Gill notes they were to prove all things said by the prophets, all the doctrines delivered — to hear them, try their gifts, attend to their doctrines, yet not implicitly believe, but examine all by the standard of the word. The witnesses note Paul charging them to prove all things and hold fast that which is good; the duty of discerning testing joined to firm holding of the good — that Paul guards the welcoming of prophecy (v.20) with the rule of discernment: the believers are not to swallow everything uncritically, but to "prove all things" (test and examine all that is taught, weighing it by the Scripture and the Spirit), and, having so tried, to "hold fast" — keep a firm and steadfast grip on — "that which is good" (the true and sound, the proven), so that openness to teaching is balanced by careful discrimination and tenacious loyalty to the truth; prove all things; hold fast that which is good. The witnesses note prove all things. The witnesses note hold fast that which is good.

The witnesses note the call to prove all things and hold fast the good — testing all teaching by the Scripture, then keeping a firm grip on the true and sound.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of discerning testing and firm holding. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to test all teaching and hold fast what proves good. The witnesses note the prove all: "prove all things" — Gill notes prove all that the prophets say, by the word; the prove all; the test; the examine; the all things; the by the word. The expositors observe the not implicitly: not to believe implicitly, but to examine; the not implicitly; the examine; the not swallow; the test; the discern. The witnesses press the by the standard: weighing all by the standard of the word and the Spirit; the by the standard; the the word; the the Spirit; the weighing; the test. The expositors draw the lesson on the hold fast: "hold fast that which is good" — keep a firm grip on the good (the true, the proven); the hold fast; the firm grip; the the good; the the true; the the proven. The witnesses observe the openness balanced: openness to teaching is balanced by discrimination; the openness balanced; the teaching; the discrimination; the careful; the test. The expositors note the loyalty to truth: and tenacious loyalty to the truth; the loyalty; the the truth; the tenacious; the firm; the hold fast. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the prove all things, the hold fast that which is good, and the testing and holding. The verse thus records the duty of discerning testing and firm holding — teaching that the believer is to test all teaching and hold fast what proves good.
22:
"Abstain from all appearance of evil." The witnesses note the charge to shun even the appearance of evil. Gill notes this concerns doctrinal evil — not only open error and heresy are to be avoided, but whatever has a show of it, looks like it, carries a suspicion of it, or may be an occasion of it; and likewise practical evil. The witnesses note Paul charging them to abstain from all appearance of evil; the avoidance of evil in every form and semblance — that Paul completes his charge to discernment with a sweeping rule of holy caution: the believer is to "abstain from" (keep away from, hold off from) not only evil itself but "all appearance of evil" — every kind and every show of it, whether in doctrine (anything resembling error or heresy) or in conduct (anything that looks like sin or might give occasion to it) — so that the Christian, having held fast the good, keeps clear even of the semblance of wrong, guarding both his soul and his testimony; abstain from all appearance of evil. The witnesses note abstain. The witnesses note from all appearance of evil.

The witnesses note the charge to abstain from all appearance of evil — keeping clear of every kind and show of wrong, in doctrine and conduct, guarding soul and testimony.

So the witnesses make the verse the avoidance of evil in every form. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to shun not only evil but its very appearance, in doctrine and conduct. The witnesses note the abstain: "abstain" — Paul charges them to abstain (keep away); the abstain; the keep away; the hold off; the shun; the avoid. The expositors observe the appearance: "from all appearance of evil" — from all appearance (every show, semblance) of evil; the appearance; the all; the every show; the semblance; the every kind. The witnesses press the doctrinal evil: Gill notes doctrinal evil — anything resembling error or heresy; the doctrinal evil; the error; the heresy; the resembling; the suspicion. The expositors draw the lesson on the practical evil: and practical evil — anything that looks like sin or gives occasion; the practical evil; the looks like sin; the occasion; the conduct; the wrong. The witnesses observe the every form: the believer keeps clear of every kind and show of wrong; the every form; the every kind; the every show; the wrong; the clear. The expositors note the guard testimony: guarding both his soul and his testimony; the guard testimony; the his soul; the his testimony; the clear; the holy caution. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the abstain, the from all appearance of evil, and the holy caution. The verse thus records the avoidance of evil in every form — teaching that the believer is to shun not only evil but its very appearance, in doctrine and conduct.
23:
"And the very God of peace sanctify you wholly; and I pray God your whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless unto the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the prayer for entire sanctification and preservation. Gill notes Paul follows his exhortations with prayer to God, knowing the saints' weakness to receive them and act on them, and his own insufficiency to impress their minds, unless the Lord himself work. The witnesses note Paul praying that the God of peace sanctify them wholly, and that their whole spirit, soul, and body be preserved blameless unto the coming of the Lord; the prayer that God himself perfect and keep the whole man — that Paul, knowing all his exhortations to holiness are vain without God's own working, turns them into prayer: that "the very God of peace" would "sanctify" them "wholly" (entirely, through and through), and that their "whole spirit and soul and body" (the complete person, in every part of his nature) be "preserved blameless" (kept faultless) until Christ's coming; the witnesses note the bearing of "spirit and soul and body" on the threefold (or twofold) constitution of man, the threefold naming marking the entirety of the person God sanctifies and keeps; sanctify you wholly... preserved blameless unto the coming of our Lord. The witnesses note the very God of peace sanctify you wholly. The witnesses note your whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless.

The witnesses note the prayer that God sanctify them wholly and preserve their whole spirit, soul, and body blameless — God himself perfecting and keeping the entire person.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer for entire sanctification and preservation. The lesson the expositors draw is that God himself must sanctify and keep the whole man blameless to Christ's coming. The witnesses note the God of peace: "the very God of peace sanctify you wholly" — Gill notes Paul prays, knowing only God can work it; the God of peace; the sanctify; the wholly; the only God; the prayer. The expositors observe the wholly: sanctify them wholly (entirely, through and through); the wholly; the entirely; the through and through; the complete; the all. The witnesses press the whole person: "your whole spirit and soul and body" — the complete person, in every part; the whole person; the spirit; the soul; the body; the every part. The expositors note the constitution of man: the witnesses note the bearing on the threefold (or twofold) constitution of man, the naming marking the entirety; the constitution; the threefold; the twofold; the entirety; the the person. The witnesses draw the lesson on preserved blameless: "be preserved blameless unto the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ" — kept faultless until Christ's coming; the preserved blameless; the kept; the faultless; the unto his coming; the kept. The expositors observe the God's working: all rests on God's own working, not the believer's strength; the God's working; the not the believer; the only God; the perfect; the keep. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the very God of peace sanctify you wholly, the your whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless, and the prayer for sanctification. The verse thus records the prayer for entire sanctification — teaching that God himself must sanctify and keep the whole man blameless to Christ's coming.
24:
"Faithful is he that calleth you, who also will do it." The witnesses note the faithfulness of God who will accomplish the work. Gill notes God calls his people into the fellowship of his Son and to his kingdom and glory, and continues to call them, not only externally by his word, but internally by his Spirit and grace. Barnes notes that, as God has begun a work of grace in their hearts, they may depend on his faithfulness to complete it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that faithful is he who calls them, who also will do it; the assurance grounded in God's faithfulness to finish his work — that Paul, having prayed for their entire sanctification and preservation, grounds the certainty of it not in themselves but in the character of God: "faithful is he that calleth you" — the God who effectually called them is faithful to his promise and purpose — "who also will do it," that is, will himself accomplish the sanctifying and keeping prayed for, so that the believer's perseverance rests on the unfailing faithfulness of the God who began the work; faithful is he that calleth you, who also will do it. The witnesses note faithful is he that calleth you. The witnesses note who also will do it.

The witnesses note the faithfulness of God who calls — who will himself accomplish the sanctifying and keeping prayed for; the believer's perseverance resting on God's faithfulness.

So the witnesses make the verse the assurance grounded in God's faithfulness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God who called his people is faithful to finish the work of sanctifying and keeping them. The witnesses note the faithful: "faithful is he that calleth you" — Gill notes God calls and continues to call by his Spirit; Barnes notes his faithfulness to complete the work; the faithful; the calleth; the his Spirit; the faithful; the his promise. The expositors observe the called: God effectually called them into fellowship with his Son; the called; the effectually; the his Son; the fellowship; the his kingdom. The witnesses press the will do it: "who also will do it" — he will himself accomplish it; the will do it; the himself; the accomplish; the the work; the finish. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty: the certainty rests not in themselves but in God's character; the certainty; the not themselves; the God's character; the faithful; the sure. The witnesses observe the the work: God will accomplish the sanctifying and keeping prayed for; the the work; the sanctifying; the keeping; the prayed for; the accomplish. The expositors note the perseverance: the believer's perseverance rests on God's unfailing faithfulness; the perseverance; the rests; the God's faithfulness; the unfailing; the sure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the faithful is he that calleth you, the who also will do it, and the assurance. The verse thus records the assurance grounded in God's faithfulness — teaching that the God who called his people is faithful to finish the work of sanctifying and keeping them.
25:
"Brethren, pray for us." The witnesses note Paul's request for the prayers of the church. Gill notes this is added with great beauty and propriety, after Paul had so earnestly prayed for them; and it is directed not to the pastors only but to all the members, whom he styles "brethren," requesting that they pray for him and his fellow labourers. The witnesses note Paul asking the brethren to pray for him; the apostle's humble request for the church's intercession — that Paul, having just poured out his own prayer for the Thessalonians, fittingly turns and asks for theirs in return: "brethren, pray for us" — a request showing both his humility (the great apostle owning his need of even the humblest believer's prayers) and the mutual duty of intercession in the body, the people praying for their ministers as the ministers pray for them; brethren, pray for us. The witnesses note brethren. The witnesses note pray for us.

The witnesses note Paul's humble request for the church's prayers — the great apostle owning his need of even the humblest believer's intercession.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's request for the church's prayers. The lesson the expositors draw is the humility and mutual duty of intercession, the people praying for their ministers. The witnesses note the request: "brethren, pray for us" — Gill notes Paul, having prayed for them, asks their prayers; the request; the pray for us; the the church; the intercession; the in return. The expositors observe the to all: directed not to pastors only but to all the members; the to all; the not pastors only; the all members; the the brethren; the church. The witnesses press the humility: the great apostle owns his need of the humblest believer's prayers; the humility; the the apostle; the his need; the the humblest; the prayers. The expositors draw the lesson on the mutual duty: the mutual duty of intercession in the body; the mutual duty; the intercession; the the body; the mutual; the prayer. The witnesses observe the people for ministers: the people pray for their ministers as the ministers pray for them; the people for ministers; the pray; the ministers for them; the mutual; the intercession. The expositors note the beauty: the request is added with great beauty, after his own prayer; the beauty; the added; the after his prayer; the fitting; the propriety. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the brethren, the pray for us, and the request for prayer. The verse thus records Paul's request for the church's prayers — teaching the humility and mutual duty of intercession, the people praying for their ministers.
26:
"Greet all the brethren with an holy kiss." The witnesses note the charge to greet the brethren with a holy kiss. Gill notes this is in opposition to an unchaste and hypocritical one; Paul's meaning is that they salute the members in his name and give his Christian love to them, recommending brotherly love and stirring them up to its mutual exercise more and more. The witnesses note Paul charging them to greet all the brethren with a holy kiss; the token of pure Christian affection and unity — that Paul, as he often does at the close of his letters, bids the believers express their mutual love by the customary greeting of that time and place, "a holy kiss" — a kiss marked by purity and sincerity (opposed to any unchaste or feigned salute), the outward sign of the warm, undissembled brotherly affection that should bind the whole church together — so that all the brethren, without exception, are to be embraced in this token of holy love; greet all the brethren with an holy kiss. The witnesses note greet all the brethren. The witnesses note with an holy kiss.

The witnesses note the charge to greet all the brethren with a holy kiss — the customary token of pure, undissembled Christian affection binding the church together.

So the witnesses make the verse the token of pure Christian affection. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believers are to express their mutual love by the holy, sincere greeting of brotherly affection. The witnesses note the greet: "greet all the brethren" — Paul bids them greet all the brethren; the greet; the all; the the brethren; the salute; the embrace. The expositors observe the all: all the brethren, without exception; the all; the without exception; the every one; the the church; the embraced. The witnesses press the holy kiss: "with an holy kiss" — Gill notes a holy kiss, opposed to an unchaste or hypocritical one; the holy kiss; the holy; the pure; the not unchaste; the not feigned. The expositors draw the lesson on the customary greeting: this was the customary greeting of that time and place; the customary; the the greeting; the the time; the the place; the the custom. The witnesses observe the pure affection: the kiss is the outward sign of pure, undissembled brotherly affection; the pure affection; the the sign; the undissembled; the brotherly; the love. The expositors note the binds together: this affection binds the whole church together; the binds together; the the whole church; the unity; the love; the bound. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the greet all the brethren, the with an holy kiss, and the token of affection. The verse thus records the token of pure Christian affection — teaching that the believers are to express their mutual love by the holy greeting of brotherly affection.
27:
"I charge you by the Lord that this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren." The witnesses note Paul's solemn charge that the letter be read to all. Gill notes this is in the form of an oath, a very solemn one — "I adjure you by the Lord" — and shows that oaths may be used on certain solemn occasions. Barnes notes it is equivalent to binding persons by an oath. The witnesses note Paul solemnly charging, by the Lord, that the epistle be read to all the holy brethren; the solemn command for the letter's public reading — that Paul, with unusual solemnity, lays it upon them as under an oath ("by the Lord") that "this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren" — that none be left out, but the whole church hear it — showing both his sense of the importance and authority of the letter (as carrying the word of the Lord), and his care that all the people, not a few only, should have the benefit of its instruction and comfort; that this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren. The witnesses note I charge you by the Lord. The witnesses note that this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren.

The witnesses note Paul's solemn charge, by the Lord, that the epistle be read to all the brethren — showing its authority and his care that all hear its instruction.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn command for the letter's reading. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul, as under an oath, charges that the whole church hear the letter, none left out. The witnesses note the charge: "I charge you by the Lord" — Gill notes this is a solemn oath, "I adjure by the Lord"; the charge; the by the Lord; the the oath; the adjure; the solemn. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes it binds them by an oath; the binds; the an oath; the solemn; the adjure; the charge. The witnesses press the read to all: "that this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren" — that the letter be read to all; the read to all; the the epistle; the all; the the brethren; the none left out. The expositors draw the lesson on the authority: this shows the letter's importance and authority, carrying the word of the Lord; the authority; the importance; the the word of the Lord; the the letter; the carrying. The witnesses observe the public reading: Paul cares that the letter have a public reading to the whole church; the public reading; the the whole church; the read; the all; the hear. The expositors note the all benefit: his care that all, not a few only, benefit from its instruction; the all benefit; the not a few; the instruction; the comfort; the all. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I charge you by the Lord, the that this epistle be read unto all the holy brethren, and the solemn command. The verse thus records the solemn command for the letter's reading — teaching that Paul, as under an oath, charges that the whole church hear the letter.
28:
"The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you. Amen." The witnesses note the closing benediction of grace. Gill notes this is the apostle's usual salutation in all his epistles, and the token of their genuineness. Barnes notes the close with the customary benediction of grace. The witnesses note Paul ending the epistle with the prayer that the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with them, sealed with "Amen"; the apostolic blessing of grace to close the letter — that Paul, as in all his epistles, closes with the great gospel benediction: the invoking of "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ" — his free, unmerited favour, with all its fruits and blessings — upon the Thessalonians, a fitting end to a letter so full of grace and comfort, and (as he notes elsewhere) the very token by which his genuine letters might be known, sealed with the solemn "Amen"; the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you. Amen. The witnesses note the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you. The witnesses note Amen.

The witnesses note the closing benediction — the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with them — the great gospel blessing fittingly ending a letter full of grace and comfort.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing benediction of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul closes, as ever, with the invoking of Christ's grace upon the church. The witnesses note the grace: "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you" — Gill notes Paul's usual closing salutation; the grace; the of the Lord Jesus; the be with you; the benediction; the blessing. The expositors observe the usual salutation: this is Paul's usual salutation, the token of genuineness; the usual salutation; the the token; the genuineness; the his epistles; the customary. The witnesses press the free favour: the grace is Christ's free, unmerited favour, with all its fruits; the free favour; the unmerited; the with its fruits; the blessings; the grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the fitting end: this is a fitting end to a letter full of grace and comfort; the fitting end; the the letter; the grace; the comfort; the close. The witnesses observe the upon the church: the grace is invoked upon the Thessalonians; the upon the church; the the Thessalonians; the invoked; the blessing; the all. The expositors note the Amen: sealed with the solemn "Amen"; the Amen; the sealed; the solemn; the so be it; the close. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you, the Amen, and the closing benediction. The verse thus records the closing benediction of grace — teaching that Paul closes, as ever, with the invoking of Christ's grace upon the church.
